Referential Integrity Handled by the DBMS

Relational databases can provide declarative referential integrity constraints, declarative foreign key rules, and triggers, which can all be used to implement Uniface relationships.

As a general rule, declarative constraints and declarative foreign key rules are preferred over the use of triggers. Triggers can be used to implement foreign key rules which are not supported declaratively by the RDBMS.

Uniface supports the following foreign key rules:

  • RES—An action which would violate referential integrity in the database must fail
  • CAS—Delete the related rows in the many table when a row in the one table is deleted
  • NUL—Set the foreign key fields to NULL in the related rows in the many table when a row in the one table is deleted

The CAS and NUL foreign key rules are only supported by Uniface when deleting the ‘one’ table. Uniface does not support the CAS and NUL foreign key rules when updating the one table.

The referential integrity constraints, rules and triggers in the database must implement the following semantics:

Table 1. Types of Referential Integrity that can be Created
Action Action Type of referential integrity
Insert on many table Restrictive (RES).
Update on many table Restrictive (RES).
Update on one table Restrictive (RES).
Delete on one table Restrictive (RES), cascading (CAS) and nullify (NULL).

There are the following possibilities with respect to (compound) foreign keys:

  • All fields of the foreign key are NULL (the foreign key is said to be completely NULL). Such a foreign key is allowed.
  • One or more fields of the foreign key are NULL, and one or more fields of the foreign key are not NULL (the foreign key is said to be partially NULL). Such a foreign key is allowed. The not NULL fields must not be checked.
  • All fields of the foreign key are not NULL (the foreign key is said to be not NULL). Such a foreign key is only allowed when a row exists in the one table in which the referenced primary or candidate key is identical to the foreign key.

For information on how to generate the SQL scripts needed to let the RDBMS support the referential integrity, see the description of Open ('O') mode 4, 5 and 6.

Creation of relationships and foreign key rules on the fly is not supported. Use the Create Script utility instead.
